title: Long time dynamics of random data nonlinear wave and dispersive equations
speaker: Andrea Nahmod
abstract: Analysis and Beyond - Celebrating Jean Bourgain's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Deterministic dispersive-PDE baseline and periodic barriers
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the nonlinear Schrödinger/dispersive setting, recalls conservation laws, scaling criticality and Strichartz-based local theory, and explains why compact tori leave severe low-regularity and critical global well-posedness gaps.

proof_stages.label: Gibbs invariance as a rough conservation law
proof_stages.summary: Bourgain's random-data program is framed around invariant Gibbs measures, whose invariance can replace missing conservation laws on rough supports, together with the limitations caused by renormalization, dimension, focusing signs, and non-Hamiltonian equations.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain's one-dimensional quintic Gibbs scheme
proof_stages.summary: For the one-dimensional focusing quintic NLS, the Gibbs measure is constructed from a Gaussian reference measure and Bourgain's proof globalizes solutions by combining finite-dimensional Hamiltonian invariance with an approximation lemma.

proof_stages.label: Two-dimensional Wick-renormalized cubic and probabilistic local theory
proof_stages.summary: In two dimensions the cubic NLS must be Wick ordered, deterministic local theory is unavailable on the Gibbs support, and Bourgain obtains probabilistic local well-posedness by writing the solution as a random linear evolution plus a smoother nonlinear remainder and exploiting large-deviation estimates.

proof_stages.label: Recent random-data extensions and propagation of regularity
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains how randomization improves local theory beyond Gibbs-measure settings, then describes joint work giving almost-sure local and positive-probability global results for the three-dimensional quintic torus problem and a non-deterministic perturbation method that propagates Bourgain-type almost-sure global results to smoother data.

proof_stages.label: Energy cascades and nonequilibrium dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The talk shifts from equilibrium Gibbs dynamics to out-of-equilibrium behavior, formulating energy cascade questions through growth of high Sobolev norms and recalling Bourgain's and CKSTT's contributions to finite or unbounded orbit phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Nonequilibrium invariant measures for a resonant NLS toy model
proof_stages.summary: In joint work, a finite-dimensional CKSTT-inspired resonant model is coupled at low and high modes to heat baths at different temperatures, and existence and uniqueness of a smooth nonequilibrium invariant measure are obtained via a delicate Lyapunov-function construction over a stratified phase space.

examples.name: Periodic Strichartz epsilon-loss counterexamples
examples.note: Bourgain's one-dimensional L^6 and two-dimensional L^4 torus examples concretely show the epsilon loss in periodic Strichartz estimates and illustrate why mass-critical L^2 local theory cannot be reached deterministically.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker says Bourgain explicitly constructed periodic Strichartz examples such as the 1D L6 and 2D L4 cases showing an epsilon loss, but does not construct or analyze the counterexamples.

passes.reason: sharp periodic examples saturate the epsilon-loss threshold and show lossless Strichartz bounds fail
Near-extremizing periodic frequency examples show the no-epsilon-loss Strichartz estimates fail.
They are worst-case periodic examples forcing the Strichartz epsilon loss and refuting a lossless estimate.
They are near-sharp periodic Strichartz test cases used to rule out lossless estimates.
worst-case high-frequency torus examples forcing the periodic Strichartz epsilon loss and refuting a lossless estimate
near-sharp endpoint torus examples forcing the periodic Strichartz epsilon loss and refuting no-loss bounds
These high-frequency periodic examples are used to show no-loss Strichartz estimates fail and to make the epsilon loss sharp.
Constructed periodic solutions/data showing lossless endpoint Strichartz estimates fail, and they are sharp/saturating examples forcing the epsilon loss.
they are used to rule out lossless periodic Strichartz estimates and saturate the epsilon-loss threshold
They are high-frequency periodic examples constructed to force the Strichartz epsilon loss and refute a no-loss estimate.
Bourgain’s periodic high-frequency examples are used to refute lossless Strichartz bounds and sharply force the epsilon loss.
They are sharpness examples showing endpoint periodic Strichartz estimates fail without an epsilon loss.

examples.name: All-random cubic Fourier interaction estimate
examples.note: A typical random cubic convolution term is worked through to show that Cauchy-Schwarz loses a lattice-counting factor whereas large deviations and independence replace it by an l^2 norm, revealing why the probabilistic method closes the estimate.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs through the all-random cubic term estimate, writing the Fourier interaction sum, explaining the resonance exclusion, contrasting Cauchy–Schwarz lattice counting with large deviation estimates, and using independence to close the bound.

passes.reason: endpoint cubic interaction with all three factors randomized, used to illustrate the large-deviation gain
The all-random term is the endpoint cubic interaction with the maximal number of randomized factors, used to prove an estimate.
It is the endpoint cubic interaction with the maximum possible random factors, used constructively via independence rather than to disprove a claim.
It is a typical all-random convolution term used to illustrate a positive large-deviation estimate.
a representative nonresonant Gaussian Fourier interaction used to illustrate the large-deviation gain
an endpoint cubic interaction with all three factors random, used to illustrate large-deviation gain rather than disprove a claim
It is a representative cubic Fourier convolution term used to illustrate the probabilistic large-deviation gain.
The all-random trilinear Fourier sum is an ordinary cubic interaction used to illustrate a probabilistic estimate, not to refute a claim.
it is a representative all-random cubic interaction illustrating the large-deviation estimate
It is a representative all-random cubic interaction term used to illustrate probabilistic large-deviation estimates.
The all-random cubic Fourier convolution is a representative proof term used positively to illustrate large-deviation estimates.
It is a representative cubic random Fourier interaction used to illustrate the large-deviation estimate.

examples.name: Bourgain's perturbed one-dimensional nonlinear wave equations
examples.note: These equations are cited as concrete historical instances where Sobolev norms exhibit power-like growth, illustrating energy transfer toward high frequencies.
examples.engagement_reason: Bourgain’s perturbed one-dimensional nonlinear wave equations are cited as explicit examples exhibiting energy transition and power-like growth, without further derivation or analysis.

passes.reason: engineered 1D perturbed wave equations are cited as examples exhibiting energy transfer, not as sporadic or sharp objects
These are engineered but ordinary perturbed 1D nonlinear wave equations used to exhibit Sobolev-norm growth.
Bourgain’s perturbed 1D wave equations are presented as explicit systems exhibiting energy transfer, not as exceptional or adversarial objects.
They are constructed wave-equation examples exhibiting energy transfer rather than a sporadic or boundary object.

explicit perturbed 1D wave equations used as positive examples of Sobolev-energy transfer
Bourgain’s perturbed 1D nonlinear wave equations are cited as explicit systems exhibiting Sobolev-norm growth, not as a disproof of a stated claim.

examples.name: Three anharmonic oscillators coupled to unequal heat baths
examples.note: The Hairer-Mattingly-type heat-chain model supplies the nonequilibrium heat-bath analogy and the three-site template that motivates the final invariant-measure construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The chain of three anharmonic oscillators coupled to unequal heat baths is invoked as prior work motivating the nonequilibrium invariant measure question, but the speaker does not work through that oscillator system itself.

passes.reason: three oscillators form a minimal finite toy chain for nonequilibrium heat-bath dynamics
A three-oscillator chain is the minimal nontrivial heat-bath model with a middle oscillator mediating nonequilibrium transfer.
The three-oscillator chain is a minimal nontrivial heat-bath model, used to establish nonequilibrium invariant measures rather than as a counterexample.
The three-oscillator chain is the minimal nontrivial heat-bath chain used as a base model for nonequilibrium dynamics.
an ordinary finite nonequilibrium oscillator-chain model with unequal baths, used as a precedent rather than a refutation
the three-oscillator chain is a minimal nontrivial heat-bath model with one interior oscillator, used as a nonequilibrium model
